content.text: A variable is a named container for storing data values. In most languages, you declare them with keywords like let, const, or var.
A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. You define it once and can call it multiple times.
A loop is a control structure that repeats a block of code. Common types include for loops, while loops, and do-while loops.
An array is an ordered collection of elements. You can access elements by their index, starting from 0.
An object is a collection of key-value pairs. In JavaScript and TypeScript, objects are fundamental data structures.
A class is a blueprint for creating objects. It encapsulates data and behavior together in a single unit.
Inheritance allows a class to inherit properties and methods from another class. It promotes code reuse.
Polymorphism lets objects of different types be treated through the same interface. Methods can behave differently based on the object.
Encapsulation bundles data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit, and restricts direct access to some components.
Abstraction hides complex implementation details and shows only the necessary features of an object.
A Promise represents an asynchronous operation that will eventually complete or fail, producing a value.
Async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises, making asynchronous code look and behave like synchronous code.
A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed after an operation completes.
An interface defines a contract that classes must follow. It specifies method signatures without implementation.
A type defines the shape and behavior of data. TypeScript uses types to catch errors at compile time.
A module is a file that exports code (functions, classes, variables) for use in other files via import statements.
npm is the Node Package Manager. It manages project dependencies and provides a registry of reusable packages.
package.json is a manifest file that describes your Node.js project, its dependencies, scripts, and metadata.
Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes in source code during software development.
A branch is a parallel line of development in Git. It lets you work on features without affecting the main codebase.
A merge combines changes from one branch into another. It integrates divergent work histories.
A pull request is a way to propose changes. It lets others review your code before merging it into the main branch.
CI/CD stands for Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment. It automates testing and deployment of code changes.
Docker is a platform for building and running containerized applications. Containers package code with all its dependencies.
REST is an architectural style for web APIs. It uses HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to operate on resources.
GraphQL is a query language for APIs. It lets clients request exactly the data they need, nothing more.
A database is an organized collection of structured data. It provides mechanisms for storing, retrieving, and managing data.
SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to communicate with relational databases. It handles queries, updates, and schema management.
NoSQL databases are non-relational. They store data in formats like documents, key-value pairs, or graphs.
Caching stores frequently accessed data in fast storage to reduce latency and load on primary data sources.
Middleware is software that sits between the OS and applications, or between request and response in web frameworks.
Authentication verifies the identity of a user or system. Common methods include passwords, tokens, and OAuth.
Authorization determines what an authenticated user is allowed to do. It controls access to resources.
JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact token format for securely transmitting claims between parties.
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a security mechanism that controls which domains can access your API.
WebSocket provides full-duplex communication over a single TCP connection, enabling real-time data exchange.
Testing validates that code works correctly. Types include unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests.
TDD (Test-Driven Development) writes tests before implementation code. The cycle is: red, green, refactor.
Mocking creates fake objects that simulate real dependencies in tests, isolating the code being tested.
Linting analyzes code for potential errors and style issues. Tools like ESLint enforce consistent coding standards.
TypeScript adds static types to JavaScript. It catches type errors at compile time rather than runtime.
A generic creates reusable components that work with multiple types while maintaining type safety.
A union type represents values that can be one of several types, written as Type1 | Type2.
A tuple is a fixed-length array where each element has a specific type. Useful for returning multiple values.
A decorator is a special function that modifies classes or their members. It uses the @ syntax.
A Map is a collection of key-value pairs where keys can be any type, unlike plain objects where keys are strings.
A Set is a collection of unique values. Adding a duplicate value has no effect.
A generator is a function that can pause and resume execution, yielding multiple values over time.
A Proxy wraps an object and intercepts operations like property access, assignment, and function calls.
A Symbol is a unique, immutable primitive value. It is often used as object property keys to avoid collisions.
Tree shaking eliminates unused code from bundles. It relies on ES module static import/export analysis.
Code splitting divides your bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand, improving initial page load time.
SSR (Server-Side Rendering) renders web pages on the server, sending fully formed HTML to the browser.
